summary: Spolsky argues that software must be designed assuming users won't read manuals, instructions, or even dialog boxes. He demonstrates through examples from Juno and Windows that verbose UI text actually reduces comprehension and usability, since most users — both novice and advanced — skip instructions entirely. He emphasizes that many users are intimidated by computers and will second-guess themselves when software questions their choices. The core design principle is to minimize words, eliminate unnecessary confirmations, and make software usable without any reading at all.
key_points: Users don't have manuals and wouldn't read them even if they did — software must be self-explanatory
More words on a dialog box means fewer people will read it; verbose instructions confuse novice users and are skipped by advanced ones
Computer-intimidated users interpret confirmation dialogs as the computer questioning their judgment, causing them to make wrong choices
There is a critical difference between software that is 'possible to use' and software that is 'easy to use' — designing for the distracted beginner benefits everyone
Domain experts who write UI text (like an Ivy League English major CEO) often make it worse by adding prose that feels helpful to them but overwhelms average users
money_quotes: Users don't have the manual, and if they did, they wouldn't read it.
Designing with these ideas in mind is called respecting the user, which means, not having much respect for the user.
You're only getting about 30% of their attention, so you have to make do with a user who, from inside the computer, does not appear to be playing with a full deck.
Users do just-in-time manual reading, on a strictly need-to-know basis.
Experience shows that the more words you put on that dialog box, the fewer people will actually read it.
The very fact that Juno was questioning her choice made her immediately assume that she was doing something wrong.
Even adding the word 'please' to a dialog, which may seem helpful and polite, is going to slow people down.
Hotel bathtubs have big grab bars. They're just there to help disabled people, but everybody uses them anyway to get out of the bathtub.
key_insight: Software should be designed so that it requires no reading to use — every word you add to the interface reduces the percentage of users who will successfully complete their task.
